title: NOC 65200 – Food and beverage servers | GTR Immigration
description: Food and beverage servers under NOC 65200 are the frontline ambassadors of Canada’s world-class hospitality and tourism industry. Tasked with managing the dining experience—from initial greetings to the professional service of complex courses and beverages—these TEER 5 professionals are essential to the operations of high-end restaurants, luxury hotels, and busy urban bistros. In 2026, as Canada continues to expand its international tourism profile, this role has become a vital entry point for skilled international workers, offering a direct path to Permanent Residency (PR) through specialized Provincial Nominee Programs (PNPs).

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category6 – Sales and service occupationsTEER Level5Major Group65 – Sales and service support occupationsSub-Major Group652 – Support occupations in food, accommodation and tourismMinor Group6520 – Food support occupationsNOC Code65200

duties: Greet patrons, present menus, make recommendations, and answer questions regarding food and beverages.
Take orders and relay them to kitchen and bar staff using digital Point of Sale (POS) systems.
Recommend wines that complement patrons' meals (Sommeliers/Wine Servers).
Serve food and beverages to patrons in a professional and timely manner.
Prepare and serve specialty foods (e.g., flambé, carving) at patrons' tables in formal settings.
Present bills to patrons and process payments via cash, credit, or debit.
Maintain tables and dining areas to ensure a clean and welcoming environment.
employment_requirements: No formal education is usually required, though completion of secondary school is often preferred.
Responsible Beverage Service Certification: Mandatory in all provinces for those serving alcohol (e.g., Smart Serve in Ontario, Serving It Right in BC).
On-the-job training is typically provided by the employer.
Sommeliers usually require specialized courses in wine selection and service.
Formal service roles may require a one- or two-year apprenticeship or college courses in hospitality.
salary_prospects: Note: Salaries for this NOC often appear lower because they do not include gratuities (tips), which are a major component of total earnings.
job_outlook: The outlook is Stable to Favourable. While retirements are not the primary driver, the expansion of the hospitality sector and the continuous need for front-of-house staff in new developments ensure that experienced servers will remain a top recruitment priority for Canadian employers.
labour_market_demand: The demand for food and beverage servers is currently Very Good across most of Canada. While some regions show "Limited" outlook due to high existing local labor, the Hamilton-Niagara region, Greater Vancouver, and the Atlantic provinces are facing significant shortages. The industry is characterized by high turnover and rapid expansion, creating a continuous stream of openings for newcomers who possess strong interpersonal skills and professional service experience.
High Tip Potential: In Canada’s service culture, base wages are often significantly augmented by tips, often doubling the effective hourly rate in high-end establishments.
Professional Growth: Excellent stepping stone to become a Food Service Supervisor (NOC 62020) or Restaurant Manager (NOC 60030).
Cultural Diversity: Work in a multicultural environment that values international service standards and language skills.
PR Opportunities: One of the most common occupations used to qualify for "Semi-Skilled" immigration streams.
pathways: BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled (ELSS): A highly popular route requiring 9 months of work experience with a BC employer.
Alberta Tourism and Hospitality Stream: A dedicated pathway for workers in the hospitality sector to transition to PR.
Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): For servers with job offers in the Atlantic provinces (NS, NB, PEI, NL).
Manitoba Skilled Worker Stream: Accessible for those who have worked in the province for at least 6 months.
